nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef slopes, reef flats, lagoons, and hard-bottom subtidal zones in clear-water habitats; tends to favor horizontal reef-floor structures and back-reef slopes with moderate laminar tidal flow; rarely found in turbid or heavily wave-exposed settings

decorPreferences: open vertical space for branching growth
horizontal slab or flat rockwork base preferred
spacing from adjacent corals to allow expansion

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Has been reported to benefit from occasional supplemental feedings of phytoplankton, fine powdered coral foods, and amino acids; tends to be poor at capturing zooplankton or larger food particles. Supplemental feeding is generally considered optional rather than required in well-lit systems.

goodWith: most soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: sensitive SPS corals placed in close proximity

imageCandidates.notes: Alexander Vasenin; 2423x3230. Whole in-situ Litophyton arboreum colony on a rock pinnacle against blue water, sharp, vibrant green translucent trunk with cream polyp clusters showing the classic soft tree/Nephthea form; best single-species representative.
ajott; 810x1200. Large creamy-yellow soft tree colony on a Red Sea reef wall surrounded by orange anthias; striking colorful in-water scene, well-lit, correct broccoli-tree ID.
Derek Keats; 1024x686. In-situ Litophyton arboreum showing distinct trunk splitting into branches with cream polyp clusters; whole colony in frame, good detail of the arborescent structure.
Jean-Paul Boerekamps; 1536x2048. Cream/tan soft tree coral, whole colony with clear polyp-cluster branch tips; public-domain, adds a different columnar form.
Jean-Paul Boerekamps; 1817x2048. Tall pale-cream soft tree colony against blue water; slightly hazy but whole subject shown, public-domain, adds form variety.
Christian Berg; 2048x1536. Tan broccoli-form soft tree coral in natural reef context; correct ID, shows the typical trade 'Broccoli Coral' appearance.

summary: The Nephthea Coral is a photosynthetic soft tree coral from the Indo-Pacific, sold under the scientific name Litophyton sp. in current taxonomy. Generally considered more beginner-friendly than most corals once acclimated, it tends to grow quickly into branching colonies under moderate light and flow.
careNotes:
TAXONOMY NOTE: The aquarium trade still widely uses the genus name Nephthea, but a molecular and morphological revision formally adopted around 2012 synonymized Nephthea with the earlier-named genus Litophyton Forskål, 1775. The current accepted name for these corals is Litophyton sp. Many retailer listings continue to use Nephthea or the abbreviated spelling 'Nepthea'.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION FROM DENDRONEPHTHYA: Nephthea Corals are zooxanthellate — they host symbiotic zooxanthellae algae and derive the majority of their energy from light. This is the fundamental difference from the superficially similar Dendronephthya (also sold as Carnation Coral or Cauliflower Coral), which is azooxanthellate (non-photosynthetic) and requires heavy particulate feeding to survive. In the aquarium trade, Dendronephthya is widely considered extremely difficult to keep alive long-term, while Nephthea is generally regarded as significantly more forgiving. Do not confuse the two based on appearance alone.

Naming confusion: Some vendors label 'Cauliflower Coral' for Dendronephthya and some for Nephthea. Nephthea is also sometimes confused with Kenya Tree Coral (Capnella sp.), which belongs to a different genus in the same family.

New specimens should be introduced carefully and given time to settle before drawing conclusions about their health.

SHEDDING BEHAVIOR: These corals periodically shed a waxy or mucus-like outer coating as a natural cleaning process. Adequate water flow assists shedding. A coral that appears deflated or 'gooey' for 1–3 days may simply be shedding — this is normal behavior and is not necessarily a sign of illness.

CHEMICAL WARFARE: Nephthea Corals release allelopathic compounds (terpenes) that can irritate neighboring corals, particularly sensitive SPS corals. Running activated carbon and maintaining physical spacing of at least 10–15 cm from adjacent colonies is generally advised.

LIGHTING: Tends to extend polyps better under moderate rather than intense light. Specimens placed in higher-intensity zones should be acclimated gradually over several weeks. Under extreme illumination, the colony may darken as zooxanthellae density increases.

FLOW: Best placed in moderate, non-turbulent flow — brisk enough to keep the branches gently swaying but not in crashing wave action. These corals are generally considered to do best in moderate laminar or gyre-type currents rather than strong, direct flow.

GROWTH: Widely reported as a fast-growing soft coral. Colonies sold as small frags (2–5 cm) can develop into sizable branching colonies within months under good conditions. This growth rate makes occasional pruning likely in smaller tanks.

WATER PARAMETERS: Generally considered tolerant of a wider parameter range than SPS corals, though stable natural seawater-type parameters are still advisable. A target alkalinity of 8–10 dKH is broadly recommended for reef systems housing these corals.
breedingNotes: Fragging is commonly practiced and straightforward — branches can be cut with scissors or bone cutters and attached to frag plugs or rubble. The fast growth rate makes Nephthea Coral one of the more commonly traded soft coral frags in the reef hobby. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning in the wild but is not deliberately practiced in captivity.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Koji Pink Nephthea
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, lighting PAR range, flow, placement, and care level. Scientific name listed as Nephthea chabrolii on product page; updated to Litophyton sp. per current taxonomy.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Nepthea Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters. Temperature 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), salinity 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm, lighting 150–250 PAR. Slight disagreement on temperature upper bound and kH range vs. ECC listing; averaged for final values.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – Keeping Nephthea Corals
additional.notes: Confirmed zooxanthellate (photosynthetic) status, Indo-Pacific native range, phytoplankton feeding notes, acclimation sensitivity, and shedding behavior. Care level rated beginner-friendly once established. Noted photosynthetic distinction from Dendronephthya.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Neon Green Nephthea
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef), flow requirements, feeding mode. Lighting reported as 50–150 PAR (lower than BRS); BRS PAR range adopted as broader consensus. Confirmed allelopathic terpene release.

additional.name: Reeflex – Litophyton arboreum
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Litophyton arboreum Forskål, 1775), native range expansion (Red Sea, Egypt, Israel, Saudi Arabia, Papua New Guinea, New Caledonia), depth range (1–24 m), temperature (24–28°C), and minimum tank size indication (~300 L). Colony size up to 80 cm from this source.
